task: Parse an AWS CloudFormation template in JSON format and extract all resource logical IDs
solution_criteria:
- The function must accept a single string parameter annotated with type hints (e.g., `template_str: str`) and return a `list[str]` of logical IDs
- The function must validate that the input is a non-None string before attempting to parse; raise a `TypeError` with a descriptive message if not
- The function must catch `json.JSONDecodeError` when parsing malformed JSON and raise a `ValueError` with a descriptive message
- The function must validate that the parsed object contains a top-level `Resources` key that maps to a non-empty dictionary; raise a `ValueError` if missing or invalid
- The function must return a list of all keys from the `Resources` dictionary as the logical IDs
- The function must return an empty list if the `Resources` dictionary exists but contains no entries
- The function must include a docstring describing the expected input format, return value, and all exceptions that may be raised

task: Write a Python function to validate AWS S3 bucket names according to AWS naming rules
solution_criteria:
The function must accept a string and return True only if the name satisfies ALL of the following AWS S3 bucket naming rules, and False otherwise:
- Length must be between 3 and 63 characters (inclusive); enforce this with a single authoritative check (either regex or explicit len(), not both redundantly)
- Only lowercase letters (a-z), digits (0-9), and hyphens (-) are allowed; dots (.) are not permitted in this implementation
- Must start and end with a lowercase letter or digit (not a hyphen)
- Must not contain consecutive hyphens (e.g., 'my--bucket' is invalid)
- Must not be formatted as an IPv4 address (i.e., reject strings matching the pattern of four dot-separated octets such as '192.168.1.1'); since dots are already excluded, explicitly document this rule is inherently satisfied and add a dedicated IP-address regex check as a safeguard
- Must include a docstring that lists each validation rule being enforced
- Must not use redundant or conflicting length checks (e.g., if the regex enforces the 3-63 length bound, do not also add a separate len() guard, or vice versa — pick one approach and apply it consistently)

task: Create a regex pattern to match valid AWS IAM role ARNs
solution_criteria:
- The regex must match all three AWS partitions: `arn:aws`, `arn:aws-us-gov`, and `arn:aws-cn`, using a named capture group for the partition
- The account ID capture group must match exactly 12 digits
- The role name capture group must allow alphanumeric characters, hyphens, underscores, and forward slashes (for paths), with a minimum length of 1 character
- The `match_arn` function must explicitly handle `None`, empty string, and non-string inputs by returning `None` or a clear sentinel value without raising an exception
- Include at least five unit tests covering: a standard AWS ARN, a GovCloud ARN, a China region ARN, an invalid ARN (wrong format), and a `None`/empty input — each test must assert the correct capture group values or confirm no match

task: Write a Python function to convert AWS EC2 instance metadata JSON response into a flattened dictionary with dot-notation keys
solution_criteria:
- Accept input as either a pre-parsed dict or a raw JSON string, using json.loads() to parse string input before processing
- Return a flat dictionary where all nested keys are joined with dot-notation (e.g., 'network.interfaces.0.mac') and leaf values are preserved as-is
- Raise a TypeError with a descriptive message if input is None or not a dict or string type
- Raise a ValueError with a descriptive message if a string input is not valid JSON
- Include a recursion depth guard (e.g., max depth of 20) that raises a RecursionError with a descriptive message to prevent infinite loops on circular or deeply nested structures
- Include a complete docstring describing parameters, return type, raised exceptions, and an example using EC2 metadata structure
- Include type hints for all parameters and the return value (e.g., def flatten_metadata(data: Union[dict, str], prefix: str = '') -> dict)

task: Create a regex pattern to extract AWS access key IDs from logs or configuration files
solution_criteria:
- The regex must use a capture group (parentheses) that wraps the entire key pattern so the full key is returned via match group(1) or equivalent
- The pattern must match all common AWS key ID prefixes: AKIA, ASIA, AROA, and AIDA (not just AKIA)
- After the 4-character prefix, the pattern must match exactly 16 uppercase alphanumeric characters (A-Z, 0-9), for a total key length of 20 characters
- The pattern must include word boundary assertions (\b) or equivalent negative lookbehind/lookahead on both sides to prevent matching keys embedded within longer alphanumeric strings (e.g., 'XAKIA...' must not match)
- The full 20-character key (prefix + 16 characters) must be captured as a single group, not split across multiple groups

task: Write a Python function to parse AWS CloudWatch log timestamps and convert them to a human-readable format
solution_criteria:
- Accept a single parameter typed as `int` with a descriptive name (e.g., `timestamp_ms`), and include a complete docstring describing the parameter, return value, and any exceptions raised
- Validate the input: raise `TypeError` if the value is not an `int`, and raise `ValueError` if the value is negative or represents a timestamp beyond a reasonable upper bound (e.g., year 9999)
- Handle `None` explicitly by raising `TypeError` with a descriptive message
- Convert the milliseconds timestamp to seconds before constructing a UTC-aware `datetime` object using `datetime.fromtimestamp(ts_seconds, tz=timezone.utc)`
- Format the output using `strftime` with an explicit format string (e.g., `"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ"`) to produce a consistent ISO 8601 string without microseconds or UTC offset suffixes
- Include type hints for both the parameter (`int`) and the return value (`str`)

task: Create a JSON schema that validates the structure of an AWS Lambda function configuration object
solution_criteria:
The JSON schema should define required fields (FunctionName, Runtime, Handler, Role) with correct data types, and must satisfy the following precise requirements:
- FunctionName must match pattern ^[a-zA-Z0-9-_:]+$ and have maxLength of 140 to support both plain names (up to 64 chars) and full ARN format (up to 140 chars)
- Runtime must be an enum restricted to valid AWS Lambda runtime strings (e.g., python3.11, nodejs18.x, java17, etc.)
- Role must match an ARN pattern such as ^arn:aws:iam::[0-9]{12}:role/.+$
- VpcConfig, when present, must require SubnetIds and SecurityGroupIds as arrays each with minItems of 1
- EphemeralStorage must be supported as an optional field with a nested Size property (integer, minimum 512, maximum 10240)
- Environment Variables keys must match pattern ^[a-zA-Z][a-zA-Z0-9_]*$ to enforce AWS naming rules (start with a letter, contain only alphanumeric or underscore characters)
- The Environment Variables object must enforce a maximum of 128 key-value pairs via maxProperties to reflect AWS limits
- Timeout must be an integer with minimum 1 and maximum 900, and MemorySize must be an integer with minimum 128 and maximum 10240

task: Write a Python function to filter AWS DynamoDB table items based on a simple attribute condition
solution_criteria:
- The function must accept a list of DynamoDB item dictionaries in boto3 typed format (e.g., `{'attr': {'S': 'value'}}` or `{'attr': {'N': '123'}}`) and a condition specified as an attribute name and a plain Python value, returning only items where the typed attribute value matches the condition value after extracting the inner typed value
- The function must support at minimum the DynamoDB types `S` (string), `N` (numeric string compared as number), and `BOOL` (boolean)
- The function must validate inputs and raise a `ValueError` if the items list is `None`, and silently skip or raise a `TypeError` for any non-dict entries in the list
- The function must include full type hints on all parameters and the return value
- The function must include a docstring describing parameters, return value, supported DynamoDB types, and any exceptions raised

task: Create a regex pattern to match and extract AWS region codes from various AWS service endpoints
solution_criteria:
The regex must match standard AWS region codes (e.g., us-east-1, eu-west-2, ap-southeast-1) as well as AWS GovCloud regions that include a three-segment format (e.g., us-gov-west-1, us-gov-east-1).
- The geographic prefix must not be hardcoded to exactly two letters; it must accommodate two-letter prefixes (us, eu, ap, sa, ca, me, af) and three-letter or longer prefixes as needed for gov-style regions.
- The region pattern must be flexible enough to match regions embedded anywhere within a dot-separated URL, including path-style S3 URLs (e.g., bucket.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com), standard service endpoints (e.g., s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com), and endpoints with additional subdomains.
- The regex must use a named capture group (e.g., (?P<region>...)) to isolate and extract only the region code from the full URL string.
- The region segment must be matched using a pattern that accounts for the variable number of hyphen-separated components (two or three), each consisting of lowercase letters or digits, rather than relying on fixed positional matching.
- The solution must not produce false positives by matching non-region dot-separated segments; the region match must be anchored or bounded by context (e.g., preceded by a dot or start of a known segment and followed by .amazonaws.com or similar).
- The regex must be implemented as a single compiled Python pattern usable with re.search() or re.findall() against a URL string.

task: Write a Python function to calculate AWS S3 storage costs based on object size and storage class
solution_criteria:
- Accept `size_bytes` (int/float) and `storage_class` (str) parameters; raise `ValueError` for negative `size_bytes` or unrecognized `storage_class` values
- Support storage classes: `STANDARD`, `STANDARD_IA`, `ONEZONE_IA`, `INTELLIGENT_TIERING`, `GLACIER`, `GLACIER_DEEP_ARCHIVE`, `GLACIER_INSTANT_RETRIEVAL`
- Apply tiered pricing for `STANDARD` (first 50TB at $0.023/GB, next 450TB at $0.022/GB, over 500TB at $0.021/GB); all other classes use flat per-GB rates
- For `STANDARD_IA` and `ONEZONE_IA`, enforce a minimum billable object size of 128KB and a 30-day minimum storage duration charge (i.e., bill for at least 128KB and at least 30/30.44 of a month)
- For `GLACIER` and `GLACIER_DEEP_ARCHIVE`, enforce a minimum billable object size of 40KB (plus 8KB overhead) and minimum storage durations of 90 days and 180 days respectively
- For `INTELLIGENT_TIERING`, include a per-object monitoring and automation fee of $0.0025 per 1,000 objects in addition to the storage rate, and document that frequent access tier is priced at $0.023/GB and infrequent access tier at $0.0125/GB (return blended or clearly documented assumption)
- Use current AWS pricing constants defined as named variables with inline comments citing the pricing tier and a note that values may become stale and should be verified against https://aws.amazon.com/s3/pricing/
- Return the total monthly cost in USD as a float rounded to 10 decimal places
